properties.title: Birth of Henry II - Le Mans (County of Maine) 1133
properties.content: Henry of Anjou is born, future King of England and founder of the Angevin Empire. (Orderic Vitalis; Gesta Stephani)
properties.attribution: medieval creator on the seal, previous to 1167, Matilda's death, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Marriage of Henry II and Eleanor of Aquitaine - Poitiers (Aquitaine) 1152
properties.content: Marriage brings vast French territories under Angevin control. (John of Salisbury)
properties.attribution: Bibliothèque nationale de France, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Treaty of Wallingford - Wallingford (England) 1153
properties.content: Stephen recognizes Henry of Anjou as heir, ending the Anarchy. (Anglo-Saxon Chronicle; William of Newburgh)
properties.attribution: Peterborough.Chronicle.firstpage.jpg: en:User:Geogrederivative work: Hchc2009, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Coronation of Henry II - Westminster Abbey (London) December 19, 1154
properties.content: Henry II crowned King of England, beginning Angevin rule. (Roger of Howden)
properties.attribution: Matthew Paris, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Treaty of Chester - Chester (England) 1157
properties.content: Scotland relinquishes claims over Northumberland, securing the northern border. (Roger of Howden)
properties.attribution: See page for author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Campaigns in Northumberland - Northumberland 1157
properties.content: Henry II fortifies royal authority in the northern marcher counties. (William of Newburgh)
properties.attribution: Rasiel Suarez, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Submission of the North / Northumbrian Barons' Submission - York/Northumberland 1157
properties.content: Henry II forces submission of northern barons (including Northumbrian lords) after reclaiming region from Scottish control; marks end of near-autonomy post-Anarchy. (Roger of Howden)
properties.attribution: Hchc2009, rowanwindwhistler, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Becket made Archbishop of Canterbury - Canterbury 1162
properties.content: Henry appoints Thomas Becket, escalating church-state conflict. (Edward Grim)
properties.attribution: Peter of  Langtoft, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Constitutions of Clarendon - Clarendon (Wiltshire) January 29, 1164.
properties.content: Henry II attempts to limit ecclesiastical privilege. (Roger of Howden)
properties.attribution: See page for author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Exile of Thomas Becket - France 1164
properties.content: Becket flees England, widening the rift with the crown. (Herbert of Bosham)
properties.attribution: Matthew Paris, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Assize of Clarendon - Clarendon (Wiltshire) 1166
properties.content: Legal reform establishing royal justice and jury presentment. (Glanvill; Roger of Howden)
properties.attribution: Original, 12th century anonymous engraver; photograph Llyfrgell Genedlaethol Cymru / The National Library of Wales from Wales/Cymru, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Murder of Thomas Becket - Canterbury Cathedral 1170
properties.content: Becket murdered by knights loyal to Henry II. (Edward Grim)
properties.attribution: Walters Art Museum, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Coronation of the Young King Henry - Westminster Abbey (London) 1170
properties.content: Henry II crowns his eldest son as junior king of England (associating him in rule but without real power, leading to later tensions). (Roger of Howden; Ralph de Diceto)
properties.attribution: See page for author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Invasion of Ireland (initial phase) - Waterford/Dublin (Ireland) 1171
properties.content: Henry II lands in Ireland, receives submissions from Irish kings, establishes lordship over parts, and grants fiefs to Anglo-Normans; marks expansion of Angevin influence from England. (Gerald of Wales; Roger of Howden)
properties.attribution: Topography_Ireland.jpg: Credit: Elevation data used in this image were acquired by the Shuttle Radar Topography Mission aboard the Space Shuttle Endeavour, launched on Feb. 11, 2000. SRTM used the same radar instrument that comprised the Spaceborne Imaging Radar-C/X-Band Synthetic Aperture Radar (SIR-C/X-SAR) that flew twice on the Space Shuttle Endeavour in 1994. SRTM was designed to collect 3-D measurements of the Earth's surface. To collect the 3-D data, engineers added a 60-meter (approximately 200-foot) mast, installed additional C-band and X-band antennas, and improved tracking and navigation devices. The mission is a cooperative project between NASA, the National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency (NGA) of the U.S. Department of Defense and the German and Italian space agencies. It is managed by NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory, Pasadena, Calif., for NASA's Earth Science Enterprise, Washington, D.C.derivative work: Hchc2009, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Compromise of Avranches -  Avranches Cathedral in 1172
properties.content: Henry II was absolved from Becket’s death and pledged troops to the Crusade at the Compromise of Avranches in 1172. (Jones, 74)
properties.attribution: Tangopaso, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons
properties.alt_text: City of Avaranches and its Cathedral

properties.title: Great Revolt of 1173–1174 - England & Normandy 1173
properties.content: Rebellion by Henry's sons and barons; northern castles play key role. (Roger of Howden)
properties.attribution: Normandy_campaign_1204.png: *France_map_Lambert-93_topographic-blank.svg: Eric Gaba (Sting - fr:Sting)derivative work: Hchc2009 (talk)derivative work: Hchc2009, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Young John Receives Castles from Henry II - Limoges, France on February 25, 1173
properties.content: In Limoges, France on February 25, 1173, King Henry II granted his 5-year-old son John the castles of Chinon, Loudun, and Mirebeau.
properties.attribution: Benjamin Smith, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Young Henry Flees Chinon to Rebel - Chinon at Christmas 1173
properties.content: In Chinon at Christmas 1173, Young Henry secretly fled at night with his brothers and knights to rebel against Henry II.
properties.attribution: See page for author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Revolt of Henry the Young King - Poitiers 1173
properties.content: Henry the Young King rebelled against Henry II, joining Louis VII of France; Eleanor and her sons Richard and Geoffrey also joined the revolt in Poitiers. (Jones, 77)
properties.attribution: Remi Jouan, CC BY-SA 3.0 <http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Eleanor Captured in Disguise - Chinon Palace 1173
properties.content: Eleanor was captured crossing France disguised as a man and imprisoned in Chinon Palace.
properties.attribution: By Franck Badaire - Fonds documentaire du Conseil Général d'Indre-et-Loire, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=14521344

properties.title: Failed Rebel Attack on Vexin -  Vexin in the summer of 1173
properties.content: Rebels attacked Vexin in the summer of 1173 without success.
properties.attribution: Siine.eu, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons
properties.alt_text: adow and woods in the vexin

properties.title: Rebels Take Driencourt - Driencourt in June and July of 1173
properties.content: The rebels successfully seized Driencourt in June and July of 1173.
properties.attribution: Cassini, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Rebels Capture Aumale - Aumale in June and July of 1173
properties.content: The rebels successfully seized Aumale in June and July of 1173.
properties.attribution: Unknown 14th-c. French artist, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Henry II Lifts Siege of Verneuil - Verneuil July of 1173
properties.content: Henry II successfully lifts the siege of Verneuil and slaughters the rebel rear guard in July of 1173.
properties.attribution: Unknown, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Scots Attack Wark -  Wark in June
properties.content: Scots allied to the rebels attacked Wark in June.
properties.attribution: By Walter Baxter, CC BY-SA 2.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=12300689

properties.title: Scottish Raids and Devastation of Northumberland - Northumberland 1173
properties.content: William the Lion of Scotland invades during Great Revolt, ravages coastal plains, besieges castles (e.g., Wark, Prudhoe), promises Northumberland to rebels; widespread destruction and looting in border areas. (Jordan Fantosme's Chronicle; Roger of Howden)
properties.attribution: The photo is not credited., Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Siege of Prudhoe Castle (first attempt) - Prudhoe (Northumberland) 1173
properties.content: Scottish forces under William the Lion besiege Prudhoe; castle holds under Odinel II de Umfraville (loyal to Henry II), frustrating Scottish claims to Northumberland. (Jordan Fantosme; English Heritage sources)
properties.attribution: Chris Tweedy / Prudhoe Castle, Prudhoe, Northumberland

properties.title: Scottish Raids near Newcastle - Newcastle-upon-Tyne during June of 1173
properties.content: Scots pillaged the countryside around Newcastle-upon-Tyne during June of 1173.
properties.attribution: Jared S. Ogle

properties.title: Battle of Fornham - Fornham near Bury St. Edmunds
properties.content: At Fornham near Bury St. Edmunds, loyalist forces defeated the rebels; many drowned in the fenland bogs.
properties.attribution: By Bogbumper - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=6426325
properties.alt_text: Soldiers were caught at River Lark

properties.title: Defense of Prudhoe Castle - Prudhoe Castle in 1173
properties.content: William’s forces attacked Prudhoe Castle in 1173, but the Umfraville family successfully defended it. Prudhoe remained the only Northumbrian castle never to fall to the Scots.
properties.attribution: By Jared S. Ogle

properties.title: Siege of Carlisle Castle - Carlisle Castle from 1173-1174
properties.content: William the Lion besieged Carlisle Castle from 1173-1174, another important fortress near the Scottish border, but he was unable to capture it. The failure to take Carlisle weakened his campaign and maintained English control over a critical border stronghold.
properties.attribution: Bill Henderson / The Keep, Carlisle Castle

properties.title: Young Henry Seizes Aumale Castle - Aumale Castle in June 1174
properties.content: At Aumale Castle in June 1174, Young Henry invaded Normandy and seized the fortress.
properties.attribution: Stephen Montgomery from Kallangur, Australia, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Neufchâtel-en-Bray Captured, Flemish Support Lost - Neufchâtel-en-Bray in June 1174
properties.content: At Neufchâtel-en-Bray in June 1174, Young Henry captured the castle but lost Flemish support when a commander was killed.
properties.attribution: Dickeybird, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Henry II Departs from Barfleur - Barfleur on July 7, 1174
properties.content: At Barfleur on July 7, 1174, Henry II set sail while Young Henry waited for favorable winds.
properties.attribution: JackyM59, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Henry II Lands at Southampton -  Southampton on July 9, 1174,
properties.content: At Southampton on July 9, 1174, Henry II landed in England.
properties.attribution: Christophe.Finot, CC BY-SA 2.5 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.5>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Henry II Performs Penance at Canterbury - Canterbury Cathedral on July 12, 1174
properties.content: At Canterbury Cathedral on July 12, 1174, Henry II visited Becket's shrine and submitted to a public flogging for atonement.
properties.attribution: Antony McCallum: Who is the uploader, photographer, full copyright owner and proprietor of WyrdLight.com, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Battle of Alnwick - Alnwick Castle on 13 July 1174
properties.content: William the Lion was captured near Alnwick Castle on 13 July 1174 by English forces under Ranulf de Glanvill. His capture ended the Scottish invasion and forced William to negotiate with Henry II.
properties.attribution: Jared S. Ogle / Alnwick Castle

properties.title: Treaty of Falaise - Falaise (Normandy) 1174
properties.content: Scotland becomes feudal subordinate to England. (Roger of Howden)
properties.attribution: This work is in the public domain in its country of origin and other countries and areas where the copyright term is the author's life plus 70 years or fewer.

properties.title: Penance of Henry II - Canterbury 1174
properties.content: Henry performs public penance to restore royal legitimacy. (Roger of Howden)
properties.attribution: Matthew Paris, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Scottish Ravaging near Alnwick / Encampment - Alnwick area (Northumberland) 1174
properties.content: William the Lion ravages Northumberland plains, encamps near Alnwick with small force before capture; exemplifies border raiding and devastation during rebellion. (Jordan Fantosme's Chronicle)
properties.attribution: J. M. W. Turner, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Battle of Alnwick / Capture of William the Lion - Alnwick (Northumberland, England) 1174
properties.content: Decisive royal victory; Scottish king ambushed and captured near Alnwick during reconnaissance amid ravaging of Northumberland; turns tide of Great Revolt and ends Scottish threat for now. (Roger of Howden; Jordan Fantosme)
properties.attribution: Canaletto, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Siege of Prudhoe Castle (second attempt) - Prudhoe (Northumberland) 1174
properties.content: William the Lion returns with reinforcements to besiege Prudhoe again; Odinel II holds out (only Northumberland castle not to fall to Scots), forcing William to withdraw before his capture at Alnwick. (Jordan Fantosme; English Heritage; Britain Express)
properties.attribution: [Knight, Charles: «Old England: A Pictorial Museum»], Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Treaty of Falaise - Falaise (Normandy) 1174
properties.content: Scotland becomes feudal subordinate to England. (Roger of Howden)
properties.attribution: Hogweard, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Treaty of Windsor - Windsor (England) 1175
properties.content: Henry II recognizes Ruaidrí Ua Conchobair as High King of Ireland in exchange for homage, formalizing Irish arrangements post-invasion. (Roger of Howden)
properties.attribution: Www.wesleyjohnston.com-users-ireland-maps-historical-map1014.gif: Electionworld (talk · contribs)IrelandVIII-FirstThirdIX-es.svg: rowanwindwhistler (talk · contribs)derivative work: rowanwindwhistler, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Expansion of Royal Forest Law - England 1175
properties.content: Forest law expanded, deeply affecting northern communities. (Assize Rolls)
properties.attribution: my work, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Assize of Northampton / General Eyre - Northampton (England) 1176
properties.content: Follow-up to Clarendon; expands travelling justices, introduces assizes (e.g., mort d'ancestor), strengthens royal justice across counties. (Assize Rolls; Glanvill)
properties.attribution: Matthew paris (Historia Anglorum), Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Emergence of de Vescy / Percy Precursors in Northumberland - Alnwick/Topcliffe (Northumberland/Yorks border) 1180
properties.content: Joscelin of Louvain (via marriage to Agnes de Percy) settles in Northumberland; early Percy lineage roots here, with Alnwick granted later; foreshadows powerful Northumbrian baronial families under Angevin rule. (Percy family histories; Pipe Rolls)
properties.attribution: Wikimandia, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Marriage of Geoffrey and Constance of Brittany - Rennes (Brittany) 1181
properties.content: Geoffrey (Henry II's son) marries Constance, securing Brittany as Angevin holding. (Roger of Howden)
properties.attribution: National Library of Wales, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Accusation of Affair Against Marshal - Fall 1182
properties.content: At an unknown location in fall 1182, five knights accused William Marshal of an affair with Queen Margaret.
properties.attribution: See page for author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Young Henry Sacks Rocamadour Shrine - Rocamadour in mid-1183
properties.content: At Rocamadour in mid-1183, Young Henry sacked the shrine for funds.
properties.attribution: unknown

properties.title: Young Henry Pillages Grandmont Priory - Grandmont Priory in mid-1184
properties.content: At Grandmont Priory in mid-1184, Young Henry looted the monastery.
properties.attribution: Daniel VILLAFRUELA, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Death of Young Henry at Martel - Martel, France on June 11, 1183
properties.content: At Martel, France on June 11, 1183, Young Henry died of dysentery.
properties.attribution: Pierre-Yves Redon (Pikendorf), Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Saladin Captures Aleppo - Aleppo in June 1183
properties.content: At Aleppo in June 1183, Saladin seized the city.
properties.attribution: Gustave Doré, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Eleanor visit Windsor -  Christmas 1184
properties.content: Eleanor's detainment loosens. She attend Christmas court at Windsor for Christmas 1184.
properties.attribution: unknown

properties.title: Geoffrey Plantagenant falls from Horse and is trampled in tournament. - Paris August 19, 1186
properties.content: Geoffrey Plantagenant falls from Horse and is trampled in tournament August 19, 1186 dying in Paris.
properties.attribution: AnonymousUnknown author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Geoffrey's son Arthur of Brittany is Born - 29 March 1187 in Nantes
properties.content: Geoffrey's son Arthur of Brittany is Born 29 March 1187 in Nantes.
properties.attribution: William Frederick Yeames, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Christian Kingdom of Jerusalem in Decline - Jerusalem between 1185 and 1187
properties.content: In Jerusalem between 1185 and 1187, the Christian Middle East teetered on the edge of collapse.
properties.attribution: Berthold Werner, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Salidin defeats Guy Lusignan at the Hotns of Hattin - Horns of Hattin on 4 July 1187
properties.content: Salidin defeats King of Jerusalem, Guy Lusignan, at the Horns of Hattin on 4 July 1187
properties.attribution: anonymous, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Siege of Tyre - Tyre late 1187
properties.content: In late 1187, following the fall of Jerusalem, Saladin attempted to seize the coastal stronghold of Tyre. Newly arrived crusader Conrad of Montferrat organized a brilliant defense, repelling both land and naval assaults. Tyre’s survival preserved a vital Christian foothold in the Levant and served as the launch point for the Third Crusade.
properties.attribution: By Jean Colombe - Sébastien Mamerot, Les Passages d’Outremer, BnF Fr. 5594, fol.423, Public Domain, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=10302776

properties.title: Fall of Jerusalem - Jerusalem October 2, 1187
properties.content: On October 2, 1187, Jerusalem fell to Saladin after the decisive Muslim victory at the Battle of Hattin, prompting Pope Gregory VIII to call for the Third Crusade.
properties.attribution: By fifteenth-century manuscript illuminator - William of Tyre, Historia and continuation (BNF Richelieu French manuscripts 68, folio 404), Public Domain, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=4068925

properties.title: Henry II Dies Soon After Hearing of His Beloved John's Betrayal - 6 July 1189 in Chinon
properties.content: Henry II after receiving the list of betrayers demanded at the final armistice meeting, Henry II is told John as the first name on the list, he wants to hear no more, dies, and has blood explode from his eyes and nose on 6 July 1189 in Chinon.
properties.attribution: See page for author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Coronation of Richard I - Westminster Abbey 1189
properties.content: Richard I crowned King of England. (Roger of Howden)
properties.attribution: Matthew Paris, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Quit claim of Canterbury - Canterbury 1189
properties.content: Richard I nullified the Treaty of Falaise at Canterbury in exchange of 10,000 Marks in 1189.
properties.attribution: By Ollamh - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=4274360

properties.title: Marriage of Richard I and Berengaria of Navarre - Limassol (Cyprus) 1191
properties.content: Richard marries Berengaria en route to Third Crusade (arranged by Eleanor); no heirs, but diplomatic tie. (Itinerarium Peregrinorum; Ambroise)
properties.attribution: Charles Allston Collins, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Siege of Acre -  city of Acre in July 1191
properties.content: The Crusaders captured the port city of Acre in July 1191 after a nearly two-year siege, dealing a major blow to Saladin’s coastal defenses.
properties.attribution: Dominique Papety, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Battle of Arsuf - September 7, 1191
properties.content: On September 7, 1191, Richard I defeated Saladin at Arsuf, showcasing superior military tactics and restoring Christian morale.
properties.attribution: By Éloi Firmin Féron - de:wiki, Public Domain, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=625841

properties.title: Treaty of Jaffa -  September 2, 1192
properties.content: On September 2, 1192, Richard and Saladin agreed to the Treaty of Jaffa, securing Christian access to Jerusalem though it remained under Muslim control.
properties.attribution: By James Grant - This file is from the Mechanical Curator collection, a set of over 1 million images scanned from out-of-copyright books and released to Flickr Commons by the British Library.View image on FlickrView all images from bookView catalogue entry for book., Public Domain, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=43190149

properties.title: Capture of Richard I - Vienna 1192
properties.content: Richard captured returning from crusade. (Roger of Howden)
properties.attribution: Peter of Eboli, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Richard I besieges Château de Châlus-Chabrol - 26 March 1199.
properties.content: Richard the Lionhearted hit by the arrow of a young boy in battle which later turned gangrenous on 26 March 1199.
properties.attribution: By Louis Boudan - Bibliothèque nationale de France, Public Domain, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=5624518

properties.title: Richard I gives the child who wounded him 100 shillings. -
properties.content: Richard the Lionhearted gives the boy who wielded the bow 100 shillings right before his death upon finding out he had slew his father and brother.
properties.attribution: By Fonquebure - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=5716785

properties.title: Richard the Lionhearted laid to rest. - Rouen
properties.content: Richard the Lionhearted had his body intombed at Fortevraud in Anjou and his Heart at Rouen.
properties.attribution: CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Marriage of King John and Isabella of Angoulême - Bordeaux (Aquitaine) 1200
properties.content: John annuls prior marriage and weds Isabella; provokes Lusignan revolt and accelerates loss of Poitou/Aquitaine influence. (Roger of Howden; Roger of Wendover)

properties.title: Battle of Mirebeau - Mirebeau (Poitou, France) 1202
properties.content: John's victory over Arthur of Brittany and Lusignans; captures rebels but leads to backlash and continental losses. (Roger of Wendover)
properties.attribution: France_map_Lambert-93_topographic-blank.svg: Eric Gaba (Sting - fr:Sting)derivative work: Hchc2009, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Loss of Normandy to France - Normandy 1204
properties.content: Philip II conquers Normandy, collapsing Angevin continental power. (William of Newburgh)
properties.attribution: France_map_Lambert-93_topographic-blank.svg: Eric Gaba (Sting - fr:Sting)derivative work: Hchc2009, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Death of Eleanor of Aquitaine - Fontrevraud Abbey 1204
properties.content: Eleanor dies, symbolizing the end of the first Angevin generation. (William of Newburgh)
properties.attribution: ElanorGamgee, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Papal Interdict on England - England (nationwide) 1208
properties.content: Pope Innocent III imposes interdict suspending church services due to John's refusal to accept Stephen Langton as Archbishop. (Matthew Paris; Roger of Wendover)
properties.attribution: Ealdgyth, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Excommunication of King John - England (nationwide) 1209
properties.content: John personally excommunicated; allows seizure of church lands but alienates clergy and barons. (Roger of Wendover)
properties.attribution: This work is in the public domain in its country of origin and other countries and areas where the copyright term is the author's life plus 70 years or fewer.

properties.title: Battle of Bouvines - Bouvines (near Lille, France) 1214
properties.content: Decisive French victory over John's coalition; seals loss of most Angevin continental lands. (Roger of Wendover; William the Breton)
properties.attribution: Horace Vernet, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Magna Carta sealed - Runnymede (near Windsor, England) 1215
properties.content: Barons force John to seal the Great Charter at Runnymede, limiting royal power, promising justice/tax consent; fails to prevent civil war but foundational. (Contemporary chronicles; original charter text)
properties.attribution: Originally uploaded by Oldie., Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Siege of Rochester Castle (by John) - Rochester (England) 1215
properties.content: John besieges and retakes Rochester from rebel barons; brutal siege during First Barons' War. (Matthew Paris; Barnwell Chronicle)
properties.attribution: Clem Rutter, Rochester, Kent, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: First Barons' War begins / French invasion - England (e.g., London/Rochester) 1215
properties.content: Barons rebel fully; Prince Louis of France invades (1216), captures key sites like Rochester; ongoing at John's death. (Matthew Paris)
properties.attribution: Chroniques de Saint-Denis, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Siege of Dover Castle (French-held) - Dover (England) 1216
properties.content: French/rebel siege fails; loyalists hold out, preventing full French conquest during First Barons' War. (Matthew Paris; Barnwell Chronicle)
properties.attribution: Henry Hulsbergh, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Death of King John - Newark-on-Trent 1216
properties.content: John dies amid civil war and baronial revolt. (Matthew Paris)
properties.attribution: Greenshed at English Wikipedia, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: True Cross Found - Jerusalem 1099
properties.content: True Cross found and enclosed in Gold in 1099 in Jersusalem
properties.attribution: This work is in the public domain in its country of origin and other countries and areas where the copyright term is the author's life plus 100 years or fewer.

properties.title: Birth of Henry II - Le Mans 1133
properties.content: Henry II was born in Le Mans, Maine in France on March 5, 1133. (Jones, 18)
properties.alt_text: Cathedral of St. Julien in Le Mans

properties.title: Knighted by King David I - Carlisle Cathedral 1149
properties.content: Henry II was knighted by his uncle, King David I of Scotland, in Carlisle Cathedral in 1149.
properties.attribution: By Diliff - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=33672644
properties.alt_text: Carlisle Cathedral in Carlisle, Cumbria, England

properties.title: William Marshall Held Hostage as Child
properties.content: William Marshall was held as a hostage by King Stephen at 5 years of age.
properties.attribution: Zacwill16, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Marriage to Eleanor of Aquitaine - Poitiers, France on May 18, 1152
properties.content: Henry II married Eleanor of Aquitaine in Poitiers, France on May 18, 1152. (Jones, 33)
properties.attribution: Hubert Clerget (1818-1899), Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons
properties.alt_text: The Église Notre-Dame la Grande, Poitiers, France

properties.title: Coronation of Henry II - Westminster Abbey December 19, 1154
properties.content: Henry II was crowned King at Westminster Abbey on December 19, 1154. (Jones, 45)
properties.attribution: Winkles, Benjamin;Garland, Robert, Architect;Moule, Thomas, 1784-1851, No restrictions, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: William Marshal Trains with William de Tancarville  - 1159
properties.content: William Marshalled trained in 1159 with his mother's cousin, William de Tancarville, chamberlain of Normandy, to become a knight at the age of 13.
properties.attribution: John Sell Cotman, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Becket Appointed Archbishop -  June 2, 1161.
properties.content: Henry II appointed friend and advisor Thomas Becket as Archbishop of Canterbury on June 2, 1161. (Jones, 61)
properties.attribution: Diliff, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Constitutions of Clarendon - the Palace of Clarendon, Wiltshire, on January 29, 1164.
properties.content: Henry II attempted to curb the Catholic Church’s power through the Constitutions of Clarendon at the Palace of Clarendon, Wiltshire, on January 29, 1164. (Jones, 56)
properties.attribution: By Jim Linwood - originally posted to Flickr as The Ruins Of Clarendon Palace, Wiltshire.Uploaded using F2ComButton, CC BY 2.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=6447114

properties.title: William Marshall Knighted - Neufchatel-en-Bray in 1166.
properties.content: William Marshall was knighted, fought valiantly, yet lost his war horse at Neufchatel-en-Bray in 1166.
properties.attribution: Andrewrabbott, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: William Marshall Gets Some Horses - Sainte-Jamme north of Le Mans later in 1166
properties.content: William Marshall  siezes two hostages and gets many war horses and smaller horses for a valuable knight in the tournament at Sainte-Jamme north of Le Mans later in 1166
properties.attribution: Poupipouw, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: William Loses His Most Beloved Horse - 1166
properties.content: William Marshall had his beloved destrier horse taken in a tournament, a slight he remembered for years not long after his wins in 1166.
properties.attribution: Master of the Codex Manesse, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: William Joinis His Uncle's Mesne -  Salisbury England from 1167-1168.
properties.content: William returns to England to join his uncle Patrick's mesne in Salisbury England from 1167-1168.
properties.attribution: Matthew Paris, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Revolt is Crushed in Poitiers - Poitiers 1168
properties.content: William, Patrick, Henry II and Eleanor crush a revolt by burning and raiding in Poitiers and the surrounding countryside in 1168.
properties.attribution: Georg Braun; Frans Hogenberg, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: William Marshal is Captured and Earl Patrick Murdered
properties.content: William, Earl Patrick Eleanor and the rest of the mesne are set up on by guy and Geoffrey. Eleanor is sent back to the castle while uncle Earl Patrick is murdered , William gives a good account of himself, but is stabbed through the thigh completely and taken prisoner.
properties.attribution: Superbenjamin, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Eleanor Escorts Young Henry to Coronation - Westminster, London in 1170
properties.content: In Westminster, London in 1170, William accompanied Eleanor back to London for her son's coronation as Young Henry.
properties.attribution: See page for author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: First Coronation of the Young King -  Westminster, 14 June 1170
properties.content: Young King Henry was coronated on 14 June 1170 at Westminster.
properties.attribution: Unknown author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: William Marshal Appointed Royal Tutor -  London, England by June 1170
properties.content: In London, England by June 1170, William Marshal was appointed head tutor and trainer of Young King Henry.
properties.attribution: -JvL-, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Henry II Nearly Dies at Domfront - Domfront, Normandy in the fall of 1170
properties.content: In Domfront, Normandy in the fall of 1170, Henry II nearly died while Young Henry and William Marshal were in England.
properties.attribution: See page for author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Murder of Thomas Becket - Canterbury Cathedral on December 29, 1170
properties.content: Thomas Becket was murdered by four knights in Canterbury Cathedral on December 29, 1170, believing they acted on Henry II’s wishes. (Jones, 73?)
properties.attribution: Canterbury Cathedral by Peter Trimming, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons
properties.alt_text: Transept of Martyrdom in Canterbury Cathedral

properties.title: Henry II Lands in Ireland - Waterford, Ireland in 1171.
properties.content: Henry II's Irish campaign begins with his landing at Waterford, Ireland in 1171. (Jones, 73)
properties.attribution: Unknown author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons
properties.alt_text: Reginald's Tower and surrounding old town of Waterford, site of Henry's landing for his Irish Campaign

properties.title: Henry II Holds Court at Bayeux -  Bayeux, France in December 1171
properties.content: In Bayeux, France in December 1171, Henry II held court.
properties.attribution: Image on web site of Ulrich Harsh., Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Henry II Returns to England -  England in 1172,
properties.content: In England in 1172, Henry II returned from abroad.
properties.attribution: Gerald of Wales, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Compromise of Avranches -  Avranches Cathedral in 1172
properties.content: Henry II was absolved from Becket’s death and pledged troops to the Crusade at the Compromise of Avranches in 1172. (Jones, 74)
properties.attribution: Tangopaso, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons
properties.alt_text: City of Avaranches and its Cathedral

properties.title: Young King Henry Weds - Winchester Cathedral on 27 August 1172
properties.content: Young King Henry weds the infante Marguerite at Winchester Cathedral on 27 August 1172
properties.attribution: See page for author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Second Coronation of the Young King - Winchester Cathedral August 27, 1172
properties.content: Henry the Young King was coronated a second time at Winchester Cathedral with Queen Margaret of France.
properties.attribution: WyrdLight.com, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Young John Receives Castles from Henry II - Limoges, France on February 25, 1173
properties.content: In Limoges, France on February 25, 1173, King Henry II granted his 5-year-old son John the castles of Chinon, Loudun, and Mirebeau.
properties.attribution: Benjamin Smith, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Young Henry Flees Chinon to Rebel - Chinon at Christmas 1173
properties.content: In Chinon at Christmas 1173, Young Henry secretly fled at night with his brothers and knights to rebel against Henry II.
properties.attribution: See page for author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Revolt of Henry the Young King - Poitiers 1173
properties.content: Henry the Young King rebelled against Henry II, joining Louis VII of France; Eleanor and her sons Richard and Geoffrey also joined the revolt in Poitiers. (Jones, 77)
properties.attribution: Remi Jouan, CC BY-SA 3.0 <http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Eleanor Captured in Disguise - Chinon Palace 1173
properties.content: Eleanor was captured crossing France disguised as a man and imprisoned in Chinon Palace.
properties.attribution: By Franck Badaire - Fonds documentaire du Conseil Général d'Indre-et-Loire, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=14521344

properties.title: Failed Rebel Attack on Vexin -  Vexin in the summer of 1173
properties.content: Rebels attacked Vexin in the summer of 1173 without success.
properties.attribution: Siine.eu, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons
properties.alt_text: adow and woods in the vexin

properties.title: Rebels Take Driencourt - Driencourt in June and July of 1173
properties.content: The rebels successfully seized Driencourt in June and July of 1173.
properties.attribution: Cassini, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Rebels Capture Aumale - Aumale in June and July of 1173
properties.content: The rebels successfully seized Aumale in June and July of 1173.
properties.attribution: Unknown 14th-c. French artist, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Henry II Lifts Siege of Verneuil - Verneuil July of 1173
properties.content: Henry II successfully lifts the siege of Verneuil and slaughters the rebel rear guard in July of 1173.
properties.attribution: Unknown, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Scots Attack Wark -  Wark in June
properties.content: Scots allied to the rebels attacked Wark in June.
properties.attribution: By Walter Baxter, CC BY-SA 2.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=12300689

properties.title: Failed Scottish Assault on Warkworth -  Warkworth in June 1173
properties.content: Scots allied to the rebels attacked Warkworth in June 1173, but failed.
properties.attribution: hayley green / Warkworth Castle

properties.title: Scottish Raids near Newcastle - Newcastle-upon-Tyne during June of 1173
properties.content: Scots pillaged the countryside around Newcastle-upon-Tyne during June of 1173.
properties.attribution: Jared S. Ogle

properties.title: Peace Talks Collapse at Gisors - Gisors
properties.content: Henry II sued for peace at Gisors, but Robert Earl of Leicester disrupted negotiations with vulgar outbursts.
properties.attribution: Gi.bareau, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Flemish Mercenaries Land at Framlingham - Framlingham September 1173
properties.content: In September 1173, Hugh Bigod enlisted Flemish mercenaries who landed at Framlingham.
properties.attribution: Framlingham Castle by Geographer, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons
properties.alt_text: Framlingham Castle from Across the River Ore

properties.title: Civilians Repel Mercenaries at Dunwich - Dunwich later in 1173
properties.content: Women and children pelted the Flemish mercenaries with rocks at Dunwich later in 1173.
properties.attribution: Stone bridge in Dunwich Wood by David Hillas, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Battle of Fornham - Fornham near Bury St. Edmunds
properties.content: At Fornham near Bury St. Edmunds, loyalist forces defeated the rebels; many drowned in the fenland bogs.
properties.attribution: By Bogbumper - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=6426325
properties.alt_text: Soldiers were caught at River Lark

properties.title: Defense of Prudhoe Castle - Prudhoe Castle in 1173
properties.content: William’s forces attacked Prudhoe Castle in 1173, but the Umfraville family successfully defended it. Prudhoe remained the only Northumbrian castle never to fall to the Scots.
properties.attribution: By Jared S. Ogle

properties.title: Siege of Carlisle Castle - Carlisle Castle from 1173-1174
properties.content: William the Lion besieged Carlisle Castle from 1173-1174, another important fortress near the Scottish border, but he was unable to capture it. The failure to take Carlisle weakened his campaign and maintained English control over a critical border stronghold.
properties.attribution: Bill Henderson / The Keep, Carlisle Castle

properties.title: Young Henry Seizes Aumale Castle - Aumale Castle in June 1174
properties.content: At Aumale Castle in June 1174, Young Henry invaded Normandy and seized the fortress.
properties.attribution: Stephen Montgomery from Kallangur, Australia, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Neufchâtel-en-Bray Captured, Flemish Support Lost - Neufchâtel-en-Bray in June 1174
properties.content: At Neufchâtel-en-Bray in June 1174, Young Henry captured the castle but lost Flemish support when a commander was killed.
properties.attribution: Dickeybird, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Henry II Departs from Barfleur - Barfleur on July 7, 1174
properties.content: At Barfleur on July 7, 1174, Henry II set sail while Young Henry waited for favorable winds.
properties.attribution: JackyM59, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Henry II Lands at Southampton -  Southampton on July 9, 1174,
properties.content: At Southampton on July 9, 1174, Henry II landed in England.
properties.attribution: Christophe.Finot, CC BY-SA 2.5 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.5>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Henry II Performs Penance at Canterbury - Canterbury Cathedral on July 12, 1174
properties.content: At Canterbury Cathedral on July 12, 1174, Henry II visited Becket's shrine and submitted to a public flogging for atonement.
properties.attribution: Antony McCallum: Who is the uploader, photographer, full copyright owner and proprietor of WyrdLight.com, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Battle of Alnwick - Alnwick Castle on 13 July 1174
properties.content: William the Lion was captured near Alnwick Castle on 13 July 1174 by English forces under Ranulf de Glanvill. His capture ended the Scottish invasion and forced William to negotiate with Henry II.
properties.attribution: Jared S. Ogle / Alnwick Castle

properties.title: Siege of Rouen Relieved by Henry II - Rouen, France on August 11, 1174
properties.content: At Rouen, France on August 11, 1174, Young Henry besieged the city, but it was relieved by Henry II.
properties.attribution: Foulques1996, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Princes Reaffirm Loyalty at Bayeux - Bayeux, France in mid-1174
properties.content: In Bayeux, France in mid-1174, Young Henry and his brothers reaffirmed their oath to King Henry II.
properties.attribution: Mairie de Bayeux, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Peace Treaty Signed at Tours - Tours on September 30, 1174
properties.content: In Tours on September 30, 1174, a peace treaty was signed granting Young Henry money and lands.
properties.attribution: Cartedaos (talk) 01:46, 14 September 2008 (UTC), CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Treaty of Falaise - December 1174
properties.content: After King William I of Scotland was captured by Henry II during the Battle of Alnwick(December 1174), the treaty forced Scotland to recognize Henry as feudal overlord. Key Scottish castles were handed over to England, and Scotland became a vassal state.
properties.attribution: Viault, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Young Henry and Marshal Settle in Poitiers - Poitiers during summer 1176
properties.content: In Poitiers during summer 1176, Young Henry and William Marshal began living independently from the king.
properties.attribution: Vermario at English Wikipedia, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Military Raids in Berry - Berry, east of Poitiers during summer 1177
properties.content: In Berry, east of Poitiers during summer 1177, Young Henry and Marshal led military forays into the region.
properties.attribution: Carlodangio, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Standoff at Anet and Sorel Border - On the border of Anet and Sorel in the late 1170s
properties.content: On the border of Anet and Sorel in the late 1170s, Young Henry and Marshal faced down a cavalry force that rode straight through them.
properties.attribution: unknown

properties.title: Marshal Crowned Champion at Pluers Tournament - Pluers, Champagne in the late 1170s
properties.content: In Pluers, Champagne in the late 1170s, Marshal attended his first tournament alone and was named best knight.
properties.attribution: Guilelmus Peraldus, Summa de virtutibus et vitiis, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Marshal Captures Knights at Eu -  Eu, Normandy on  the border with Picardy in the late 1170s
properties.content: In Eu, Normandy on  the border with Picardy in the late 1170s, Marshal captured 10 knights and 12 horses.
properties.attribution: See page for author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Marshal Defends Honor and Horse at Épernon - Épernon in the late 1170s
properties.content: In Épernon in the late 1170s, Marshal was invited to Count Theobald's home before a tournament; he later caught and beat a thief who tried to steal his horse, but asked for leniency.
properties.attribution: Poupipouw, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Return to England for Easter at Winchester - Winchester Castle,
properties.content: At Winchester Castle, Young Henry and Marshal returned to England for Easter after three years abroad.
properties.attribution: Michael D Beckwith from England, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Philip II Falls Ill After Boar Hunt - Chartres in summer 1179
properties.content: Near Chartres in summer 1179, Philip II became ill after getting lost during a boar hunt.
properties.attribution: unknown master, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Louis VII Pilgrimage to Canterbury - Canterbury in August 1179
properties.content: At Canterbury in August 1179, Louis VII visited Becket’s shrine to pray for his son’s health and met Henry II.
properties.attribution: Bibliothèque nationale de France, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Philip II's Coronation Attended by Young Henry - Reims in November 1179
properties.content: At Reims in November 1179, Young Henry and Marshal attended Philip’s coronation with 200 knights.
properties.attribution: Bibliothèque nationale de France, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Tournament of 3,000 Knights at Lagny-sur-Marne - Lagny-sur-Marne in November 1179

properties.attribution: See page for author, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Death of King Louis VII - Paris in September 1180
properties.content: In Paris in September 1180, King Louis VII died.
properties.attribution: Natalis de Wailly (1805-1886), Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Siege of Puy-St-Front -  Puy-St-Front in Périgord on July 1, 1182
properties.content: At Puy-St-Front in Périgord on July 1, 1182, the town came under siege.
properties.attribution: unknown

properties.title: Young Henry Honored in Limoges - Limoges in summer 1182
properties.content: In Limoges in summer 1182, Young Henry, now seen as a noble figure, visited the Abbey of St. Martial and gifted them a cloak marked 'Rex Henricus.'
properties.attribution: Jean Fayen et Maurice Bouguereau, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Accusation of Affair Against Marshal - fall 1182,
properties.content: At an unknown location in fall 1182, five knights accused William Marshal of an affair with Queen Margaret.
properties.attribution: See page for author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Queen Margaret Sent to Paris - Paris, France in fall 1182
properties.content: In Paris, France in fall 1182, Queen Margaret was sent away.
properties.attribution: Limbourg Brothers, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Final Tournament with Young Henry and Marshal - Gournay and Ressons later in 1182
properties.content: Between Gournay and Ressons later in 1182, Young Henry and Marshal attended their final tournament together in visible tension.
properties.attribution: Master of the Codex Manesse, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Marshal Omitted from Royal Proclamations - Cistercian abbey near Fontevraud

properties.title: Philip of Flanders Courts Marshal for Team - St. Omer in early 1183

properties.attribution: Ad Meskens, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Richard Besieges Limoges - Limoges in April 1183
properties.content: At Limoges in April 1183, Richard besieged the city where Young Henry and his allies were entrenched.
properties.attribution: Traumrune, CC BY-SA 3.0 <http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Young Henry Sacks Rocamadour Shrine - Rocamadour in mid-1183
properties.content: At Rocamadour in mid-1183, Young Henry sacked the shrine for funds.
properties.attribution: unknown

properties.title: Young Henry Pillages Grandmont Priory - Grandmont Priory in mid-1184
properties.content: At Grandmont Priory in mid-1184, Young Henry looted the monastery.
properties.attribution: Daniel VILLAFRUELA, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Death of Young Henry at Martel - Martel, France on June 11, 1183
properties.content: At Martel, France on June 11, 1183, Young Henry died of dysentery.
properties.attribution: Pierre-Yves Redon (Pikendorf), Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Young Henry’s Organs Buried at Grandmont - Grandmont Priory in late 1183,
properties.content: At Grandmont Priory in late 1183, his organs were interred there, despite his earlier desecration.
properties.attribution: GFreihalter, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Young Henry Entombed at Le Mans -  Le Mans in late 1184
properties.content: At Le Mans in late 1184, Young Henry was buried in a lead coffin.
properties.attribution: Leon petrosyan, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Young Henry’s Remains Moved to Rouen -  Rouen in late 1185
properties.content: At Rouen in late 1185, his body was exhumed and reburied.
properties.attribution: Philippe Roudaut, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Marshal Requests Pilgrimage and Royal Grant - 1183
properties.content: At an unknown location in 1183, William Marshal sought royal permission for a pilgrimage and a 100-pound grant.
properties.attribution: Text by Geoffrey Chaucer, illustrations by unknown artists (probably 3), Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

properties.title: Marshal Visits Family in England - England during midsummer 1183
properties.content: In England during midsummer 1183, Marshal visited family and settled his affairs.

properties.title: Marshal Departs for Jerusalem -  Jerusalem in September 1183

properties.content: Eleanor visits Normandy.
properties.attribution: unknown

properties.title: Geoffrey's son Arthur of Brittany is Born - 29 March 1187 in Nantes

properties.content: Phillip II of france invades the Angevin province of Berry in 1187

properties.title: Siege of Tyre - Tyre late 1187
properties.content: In late 1187, following the fall of Jerusalem, Saladin attempted to seize the coastal stronghold of Tyre. Newly arrived crusader Conrad of Montferrat organized a brilliant defense, repelling both land and naval assaults. Tyre’s survival preserved a vital Christian foothold in the Levant and served as the launch point for the Third Crusade.

properties.title: Le Mans Attacked and Successfully Beseiged -  Le Mans 12 June 1189
properties.content: On 12 June 1189, Henry II patrolled the Huisne River adjacent to Le Mans. Though a bridge was destroyed French knights and disaffected allies forded the river successfully attacking Henry II and is retinue. The city caught fire and Henry II, William Marshall, William Mandeville, John and allies were forced to relinguish the city and flee. Le Mans, the site of St. Julien Cathedral where Geoffrey Plantagenant is buried was sacked by Richard I and Phillip. Knights and mercenaries harried and cut down.

properties.title: Marshal Meets with Richard I - Fontevraud 10 July 1189
properties.content: 10 July 1189 Richard I arrived at Fontevraud and confronted William about their meeting outside of Le Mans. After a tense exchange, William offered to take William Marshal on  as an ally. He said, whereas his father merely promised Isabel de Clare to Marshal, he would make this a reality and offered her not guardianship, but her hand in marriage. He treated Henry II's loyal retainers similarly, while those who had betrayed his father, for him, he favoured much less.
